section.title: Benediction and Farewell
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes with one of the great benedictions of Scripture and a few personal words. The blessing gathers the whole message: the God of peace raised the great Shepherd from the dead by the blood of the eternal covenant, and He now equips His people to do His will. The farewell mentions Timothy, sends greetings, and ends with grace.
section.thesis: The God of peace, who by the blood of the eternal covenant raised the Lord Jesus, the great Shepherd of the sheep, is able to equip His people with everything good to do His will and to work in them what pleases Him through Jesus Christ, to whom belongs eternal glory; and so the letter ends with a plea to heed its exhortation, personal news, greetings, and grace.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the closing benediction, invoking the God of peace who raised the great Shepherd by the blood of the eternal covenant to equip His people; and the farewell, with a final appeal, news of Timothy, greetings, and a parting word of grace.

units.label: The benediction
units.note: the God of peace and the great Shepherd

verses.text: Now may the God of {{eirene|peace}}, who through the blood of the eternal {{diatheke|covenant}} brought back from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great {{poimen|Shepherd}} of the sheep,
{{katartizo|equip}} you with every good thing to do His will. And may He accomplish in us what is pleasing in His sight through Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever and ever. Amen.

units.label: Final words and greetings
units.note: grace be with all of you

verses.text: I urge you, brothers, to bear with my word of exhortation, for I have only written to you briefly.
Be aware that our brother Timothy has been released. If he arrives soon, I will come with him to see you.
Greet all your leaders and all the saints. Those from Italy send you greetings.
Grace be with all of you.

eirene.gloss: Peace, wholeness, reconciliation. God is the God of peace, the one who has made peace between Himself and His people through the blood of the covenant, and who now settles and completes His people in that peace.

diatheke.gloss: A covenant. The eternal covenant is the new covenant sealed by Christ's blood, everlasting and unbreakable; it is by this blood that God raised the Shepherd and secures His flock forever.

poimen.gloss: A shepherd. Jesus is the great Shepherd of the sheep, raised from the dead to lead, feed, and keep His flock; the title gathers the Old Testament hope of the shepherd who tends God's people.

katartizo.gloss: To equip, mend, complete, put fully in order, as a net is mended or a bone set. God equips His people with everything good to do His will, working in them what is pleasing in His sight.

basic.context: Hebrews ends with a magnificent blessing and a warm farewell. The benediction sums up the whole letter in one sentence: the God of peace raised Jesus, the great Shepherd, from the dead by the blood of the eternal covenant, and this God equips His people to do His will. Then the writer asks them to receive his exhortation, shares news of Timothy, passes on greetings, and closes with grace.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the benediction as a summary of Hebrews: covenant, blood, resurrection, priest-shepherd, and God working in us. It is also a prayer, asking God to do what the letter has urged. The farewell gives rare personal detail in an otherwise anonymous letter.
basic.originalLanguages: The God of eirēnē, peace, raised Jesus by the blood of the eternal diathēkē, covenant. Jesus is the great poimēn, Shepherd. God will katartizō, equip, His people to do His will.
basic.typology: The great Shepherd fulfills the Old Testament promise of God shepherding His flock and of a coming shepherd-king. The eternal covenant fulfills the new covenant promised through Jeremiah and sealed in Christ's blood.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Lord raised from the dead, the great Shepherd of the sheep, and the mediator through whom God works what pleases Him. The resurrection, mentioned explicitly here, crowns the letter's portrait of His finished work.
basic.god: God is the God of peace who raised Jesus and who equips His people. He works in us what is pleasing to Him, so that even our obedience is His gift, all to His eternal glory.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 63 speaks of the shepherd brought up from the sea. John 10 calls Jesus the good shepherd. Philippians 2 says God works in us to will and to act.
basic.oneStory: The letter closes by gathering its whole message into a blessing: the God of peace has raised the great Shepherd by covenant blood and now equips His people, leading His flock to glory.

context.literary: The benediction of verses 20 to 21 is a single, theologically dense sentence, one of the fullest in the New Testament, followed by a typical epistolary closing with personal notes and greetings. The mention of 'those from Italy' and of Timothy are among the few concrete personal details in the letter.
context.historical: The reference to Timothy's release and the greeting from those of Italy have fed discussion of the letter's origin and destination, though the author remains unnamed. The 'word of exhortation' in verse 22 is the writer's own description of the whole letter as a sermon in written form.
context.canonical: The benediction is one of Scripture's great trinitarian and gospel-shaped blessings, uniting resurrection, covenant, and sanctification, and it has been treasured in Christian worship as a fitting summary of the hope of the whole letter.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bless the readers by praying that the God of peace would equip them to do His will through the risen Shepherd, and to close the letter with a final appeal, personal news, and grace.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The benediction is a prayer and blessing rather than a command, yet it gathers up the letter's exhortations by asking God to accomplish them; the closing is personal and pastoral.

debates.question: Does the anonymity and the closing note affect how we read the letter's authority?

views.label: Authorship unknown, authority secure
views.case: The letter never names its author, and the early church did not settle the question; its inspired authority rests on its content and its reception as Scripture, not on identifying the writer, and it should not be assigned to Paul with confidence.

views.label: A known circle of the apostolic age
views.case: The mention of Timothy and of those from Italy places the writer within the apostolic circle, which supports the letter's authority even though the specific author remains unknown.

debates.bearing: Either way, the letter's authority stands on its inspired message; humility about the human author is appropriate, and Pauline authorship should not be asserted.

study.originalLanguages: The blessing is packed with the letter's themes. It addresses the God of eirēnē, peace, the reconciliation secured through the cross. This God raised the Lord Jesus by the blood of the eternal diathēkē, the everlasting covenant, and Jesus is named the great poimēn, Shepherd, of the sheep, the risen one who leads and keeps His flock. The prayer is that God will katartizō His people, a word for mending, completing, and fully equipping, so that they are put in working order to do His will. The whole is offered through Jesus Christ, to whom belongs glory forever and ever, sealing the letter with worship.

typology.type: God shepherding His flock in the Old Testament
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Jesus, the great Shepherd of the sheep, raised to lead God's people.

typology.type: The new covenant promised through Jeremiah
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled as the eternal covenant sealed in Christ's blood, by which God raised the Shepherd.

typology.type: The shepherd brought up from the deep waters, Isaiah 63
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in the God of peace bringing Jesus back from the dead as the great Shepherd.

study.christ: Christ is the great Shepherd of the sheep, the Lord Jesus whom God brought back from the dead. Here at the letter's close the resurrection is named explicitly, crowning all that has been said about His once-for-all sacrifice and eternal priesthood: the one who offered His blood is risen to shepherd His flock. His blood is the blood of the eternal covenant, unbreakable and everlasting, and it is through Him that God equips His people and works what pleases Him. To Christ the writer ascribes glory forever and ever, ending the letter in worship of the risen Shepherd.
study.god: God is the God of peace, the one who reconciles and makes whole, and the one whose power raised the Lord Jesus from the dead. He is the God who equips His people with everything good to do His will and who works in them what is pleasing in His sight, so that the obedience the letter has urged is finally God's own gift and work. All of it comes through Jesus Christ and returns to God as glory forever and ever. The letter thus ends where it began, with the God who acts decisively in His Son and who deserves everlasting praise.

crossReferences.note: God who brought up the shepherd of His flock, echoed in the God of peace raising the great Shepherd.
Jesus the good shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, fulfilled in the great Shepherd raised from death.
God works in you to will and to act according to His good purpose, matching God working what pleases Him in us.
The everlasting covenant God makes with His people, behind the eternal covenant in Christ's blood.

study.oneStory: Hebrews ends by folding its whole message into a single blessing. The God of peace has done what the letter has proclaimed: through the blood of the eternal covenant He raised the great Shepherd from the dead, and He now equips His flock to do His will and works in them what pleases Him. The story of the Son who spoke God's final word, purified sins, and sat down enthroned reaches its close as the risen Shepherd leads His people onward, all to the glory of God forever, with the parting word, grace be with all of you.

quiz.q: How does the benediction describe what God did for Jesus?

options.text: The God of peace brought Him back from the dead as the great Shepherd, by the blood of the eternal covenant.
options.note: Verse 20 names the resurrection of the great Shepherd.

options.text: God raised Him from the dead by His mighty power.
options.note: True and central, and the benediction adds that He did so through the blood of the eternal covenant, as the great Shepherd.

options.text: God made Him an ordinary shepherd.
options.note: He is the great Shepherd of the sheep, not merely ordinary.
The resurrection is by the blood of the eternal covenant, not its abandonment.

quiz.q: What does the writer pray God will do for the readers?

options.text: Equip them with every good thing to do His will and work in them what pleases Him.
options.note: Verse 21 is the heart of the prayer.

options.text: Work in them what is pleasing in His sight.
options.note: This is genuinely part of the prayer, which also asks God to equip them with every good thing to do His will.
The prayer is for equipping to do God's will, not for comfort.
It goes beyond forgiveness to God equipping and working in them.

quiz.q: What title for Jesus does the benediction use, and what does it convey?

options.text: The great Shepherd of the sheep, who leads and keeps His flock.
options.note: Verse 20 calls Him the great Shepherd, raised to tend His people.

options.text: The Shepherd raised from the dead for His flock.
options.note: True, He is the risen Shepherd, and the specific title used is the great Shepherd of the sheep, supreme over the flock.
He is the great Shepherd, supreme over the flock.

options.text: A distant owner uninvolved with the sheep.
options.note: He is the Shepherd who tends and keeps His sheep.

quiz.q: How does the writer describe his own letter in verse 22?

options.text: A word of exhortation, written briefly, which he urges them to bear with.
options.note: Verse 22 calls the letter a word of exhortation.

options.text: A serious message he hopes they will take to heart.
options.note: True in tone, and specifically he calls it a word of exhortation, written only briefly, that he urges them to bear with.

options.text: A private message for one person.
options.note: It is addressed to the whole community.

options.text: An exhaustive treatise leaving nothing out.
options.note: He says he has written only briefly, not exhaustively.

questions: God equips you with everything good to do His will. Where do you need to trust Him to supply what obedience requires rather than relying on your own strength?
Jesus is the great Shepherd raised from the dead. How does knowing your Shepherd is alive and leading you shape your week?
God works in His people what is pleasing in His sight. How does it free you to know your obedience is finally His gift and work?
The letter is a word of exhortation to be heeded. Having studied Hebrews, what one truth most calls for a change in how you live?
The letter ends with grace. How does resting in God's grace, rather than your performance, send you out to serve Him with joy?

translationNotes.note: 'Brought back from the dead' is the one explicit mention of the resurrection in the letter; it crowns the earlier emphasis on Christ's finished sacrifice and enthronement.
'Equip' translates katartizō, used of mending nets or setting a bone; it pictures God putting His people fully in order to do His will.

'Those from Italy send you greetings' is ambiguous, and may mean either the writer is in Italy or that Italian believers away from home send greetings back; it does not settle the letter's origin, and the author remains unnamed.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 13:20-25. No em dashes occurred in this passage. The authorship of Hebrews is unknown and is not asserted as Pauline. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
